text: Footstrike is the major contributor to hemolysis during running, causing a fourfold greater increase in plasma free hemoglobin compared to cycling at equivalent oxygen uptake.
One hour of running at 75% VO2max causes significant hemolysis as measured by increased plasma free hemoglobin and decreased serum haptoglobin.
General circulatory stress from exercise (independent of footstrike) also contributes to hemolysis, but to a much lesser degree than mechanical foot impact.
Oxidative stress to red blood cells (measured by methemoglobin) increases equally during running and cycling, indicating it is not footstrike-dependent.
specific_value: 2x increase in methemoglobin for both modes

text: Runners may be at greater risk for iron depletion than cyclists or swimmers due to repeated footstrike-induced red blood cell destruction, warranting closer monitoring of iron status.
Serum haptoglobin decreases significantly after one hour of running but not after equivalent-intensity cycling, confirming footstrike as the primary mechanical cause of exercise-induced hemolysis.
